title: Rip Jig for Circular Saw
summary: A shop-made straightedge jig that turns a circular saw into a reliable ripping tool — build once, trim to your own saw's base plate, then clamp and rip any board to width.
description:
A circular saw can rip a board to width freehand, but the cut wanders without a fence to ride against — this jig is that fence, built once and calibrated to your specific saw. A strip of plywood carries a 1x3 fence, and the saw's base plate rides directly against the 1x3 while the blade trims the plywood's overhanging edge flush — that self-trimming step is what calibrates the jig to your particular saw's blade offset, rather than a generic measurement that would be wrong on a different saw.

Elevate the plywood on scrap blocking or foam before making the calibration cut, fully supporting it so the offcut can't pinch the blade as it separates — a pinched blade in a rip cut can kick back hard. Trim one edge, then flip and trim the second edge the same way, this time running the larger portion of the saw's base plate alongside the 1x3 rather than the narrow side, since a rip jig needs a clean reference edge on both sides to be usable in either orientation.

To use the finished jig: mark your cut line on both ends of the workpiece, elevate the workpiece the same way you elevated the jig during calibration, then clamp the jig in place with the jig positioned OVER the piece you're keeping — the saw runs over the scrap side, so any minor drift lands in the waste, not your finished piece.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood or underlayment, 8 ft x 12 in
Scrap wood or foam, for elevating stock

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood or underlayment, 8 ft x 12 in

steps.title: Attach the fence
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 fence to the 1/4" plywood base with 3/4" fasteners and wood glue.
materials: 1/4" plywood or underlayment, 8 ft x 12 in

steps.title: Calibrate the first edge
steps.body: Set the circular saw blade depth 1/4" past the thickness of the plywood. Elevate the plywood fully on scrap blocking or foam so the cut piece can't pinch the blade. Run the saw down the 1x3, trimming the excess plywood flush.
materials: 1/4" plywood or underlayment, 8 ft x 12 in

steps.title: Calibrate the second edge
steps.body: Repeat on the other side of the 1x3, this time running the larger portion of the saw's base plate alongside the fence.
materials: 1/4" plywood or underlayment, 8 ft x 12 in

steps.title: Use the jig
steps.body: Mark your desired cut line on both ends of the workpiece. Elevate the workpiece on scrap or foam. Clamp the jig in place matching your pencil marks, with the jig positioned over the piece you're keeping — the saw runs over the scrap side. Set the blade depth 1/4" past the material thickness and rip.
materials: Scrap wood or foam, for elevating stock
